paper_title: RankJudge: A Multi-Turn LLM-as-a-Judge Synthetic Benchmark Generator

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: As interactive LLM-based applications are created and refined, model developers need to evaluate the quality of generated text along many possible axes. For simpler systems, human evaluation may be practical, but in complicated systems like conversational chatbots, the amount of generated text can overwhelm human annotation resources. Model developers have begun to rely heavily on auto-evaluation, where LLMs are also used to judge generation quality. However, existing LLM-as-a-judge benchmarks largely focus on simple Q\&A tasks that do not match the complexity of multi-turn conversations. We introduce RankJudge, a benchmark generator for evaluating LLM-as-a-judge on multi-turn conversations grounded in reference documents. RankJudge creates pairs of conversations where one conversation has a single flaw injected into one turn. This construction allows paired conversations to be labeled unambiguously as better or worse, and precisely isolates failure categories to individual turns, enabling a strict joint correctness criterion for judging. We implement RankJudge across the domains of machine learning, biomedicine, and finance, evaluate 21 frontier LLM judges, and rank those judges via the Bradley-Terry model. Our formulation also allows ranking each conversation pair with difficulty ratings, which we use to dynamically curate the evaluation slice to reduce label noise, as confirmed via human annotation. We find that judge rankings are stable under partial observability, coarser correctness criteria, and an alternative random-walk rating algorithm.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large language models are increasingly evaluated by other large language models (LLMs). Pairwise judging [1, 2] has become the dominant scalable substitute for human preference collection. As judge models are now used to score training data, gate releases, and rank checkpoints, judge quality has itself become central assumption. leaderboard built on weak judge may silently reward the wrong behavior. Stress-testing the judges themselves is therefore first-order problem, and one that existing judge benchmarks address only partially. Current judge benchmarks have recurring shortcomings: the dialogues that real-world LLM assistants produce are multi-turn and referencegrounded, while most judge benchmarks score isolated single-turn responses. Failure modes that matter in deployment, such as later turn contradicting an earlier one or content-level constraint silently dropping after several turns [3, 4], simply cannot surface in the single-turn setting. Another shortcoming is that verdict-only correctness conflates picked the right side with understood why: judge that prefers the better conversation while misattributing the flaw to the wrong turn or category has reached the right conclusion through the wrong reasoning, and existing leaderboards cannot tell Work done during an internship at Layer 6 AI. Preprint. Figure 1: Overview of RANKJUDGE, benchmark generator for multi-turn judge evaluation. the two apart. Lastly, static accuracy on fixed pool offers no principled way to identify which items actually separate strong judges from weak ones [5, 6, 7]. In this paper, we introduce RANKJUDGE, benchmark generator for multi-turn, reference-grounded judge evaluation. Each item is pair of conversations sampled independently from the same reference document under two conditioning axes: user behavior archetype, and targeted assistant failure type, with the failure injected into exactly one turn of the worse branch. As shown in Figure 1, because the flaw is preconstructed by the generator, the ground-truth tuple of better conversation, flawed turn, and failure category is uniquely determined per item from the generation prompt itself, before any judge sees the pair. At evaluation time, we ask each judge for joint prediction over verdict, turn, and type, and credit it only when all three components match. This consistency check distinguishes correct judgments from correct guesses. The benchmark construction is fully synthetic, with no per-item human label required, which lets us scale coverage densely and regenerate the pool deterministically whenever the generator or verifier is upgraded. We ensure the accuracy of our labels using two complementary methods. First, three-layer automated verifier cascade checks for coherence, adherence, and grounding, and only keeps pair of conversations when the targeted flaw is isolated to the correct turn. Additionally, every other claim in both conversations must be fully supported by the source. Second, we use the Bradley-Terry model to analyze how the judges scored the test pairs [2]. This gives us calibrated difficulty rating for each pair and allows us to dynamically curate polished evaluation slice by removing the items with the very highest difficulty scores, i.e., top-Elo tail. Both human audit and held-out fine-tuning experiment independently flagged this tail as the subset containing label noise. We apply RANKJUDGE to produce three benchmarks in distinct knowledge-intensive domains: Machine Learning, Biomedicine, and Finance, and evaluate 21 frontier judges spanning proprietary and open-weight families on each. The leaderboard separates judges across nearly 1200 Elo points, and several open-weight checkpoints outrank frontier proprietary judges. The bipartite framing also admits partial observability, so judges can be scored on different subsets of pairs while retaining their positions on the same scale, which lowers the required compute. The resulting ranking is stable under match subsampling, under coarser correctness criterion, and under an Empirical Interaction Propagation (EIP) cross-check [8]. RANKJUDGE also surfaces model-capability ceiling: weaker judges collapse their predictions onto single failure category rather than scattering across the taxonomy, and targeted prompt rewrites of mid-ranked frontier judge fail to lift it onto the accuracy-cost Pareto frontier, exposing capability gap that prompting cannot close. We summarize our contributions as follows: RANKJUDGE is benchmark generator for multi-turn, reference-grounded judge evaluation whose ground-truth verdict, flawed turn, and failure type are specified in the generation prompt and then scored under joint correctness criterion. semi-automated discovery loop surfaces multi-turn assistant failure types, and dual-conditioned generation independently simulates user-behavior and assistant-failure axes. Construction is fully synthetic; three-layer automated verifier and Elo-based curation of the high-difficulty tail are validated by human audit and held-out fine-tuning experiment that independently flag substantially overlapping noisy slice. 2 Instantiations in Machine Learning, Biomedicine, and Finance produce leaderboards spanning proprietary and open-weight judge families, which remain stable under various conditions, and surface systematic class bias in weaker judges.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Multi-turn LLM Benchmarks. LLM evaluation has shifted from single-turn benchmarks like MMLU [5] and GSM8K [9], which miss the user-model-environment dynamics that drive real-world utility [10, 11], to multi-turn frameworks [1, 12, 13, 14] that probe correctness, helpfulness, and interactive patterns [15]. consistent finding emerges across these works: single-turn ability does not transfer to multi-turn success [10], and frontier models degrade sharply across turns due to compounding unreliability [4]. These dynamics motivate our focus on multi-turn, reference-grounded conversations as the setting in which judge quality must itself be stress-tested. LLM-as-a-Judge. Reward models are crucial for aligning and improving the capabilities of LLMs [16, 17]. The traditional scalar reward model [18] gives single verdict indicating the response quality. However, scalar models suffer from certain limitations, for example they are vulnerable to hacking [19], and lack the ability to localize or categorize specific errors. LLMs have demonstrated strong capability to mimic human reasoning and evaluate inputs based on predefined criteria while being scalable and effective. The concept of LLM-as-a-judge [1, 20, 21] has become widely used for tasks like providing rich reward signals for LLM alignment [22], producing chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning along with final judgment as evaluators [23, 24], and data annotation [25, 26]. These judges can be implemented either via direct prompting of general-purpose LLMs [1, 27] or as specialized fine-tuned evaluators [28, 29]. Existing frameworks typically adopt either pointwise or pairwise evaluation protocols. Pointwise methods [21, 23] score responses independently, while pairwise methods [1, 28] compare responses to predict relative preferences. RankJudge Turn Drv. Usr. Doc. Diag. Pipe. Eval. Rank LLMBar [30] JudgeBench [31] ContextualJudgeBench [32] DHP [33] ReIFE [34] JuStRank [35] JudgeArena [36] MEDAL [37] Acc. Acc. Acc. Disc. Acc. Rank Rank Acc. Benchmarking LLM-as-a-Judge. MTBench [1, 38] helped establish LLM-as-a-judge evaluation for chat assistants by reporting agreement with humans. Initial works on metaevaluation of judges focused on single-turn settings: LLMBar [30] uses natural and adversarial pairwise examples, DHP [33] measures natural language generation evaluation capabilities using perturbations, ReIFE [34] varies LLMs, protocols and datasets. JudgeBench [31] converts factuality and correctness datasets into benchmarks for meta-evaluation, JuStRank [35] studies judges through systems level ranking agreement with human rankings, and ContextualJudgeBench [32] grounds evaluation in external documents. Other works study LLM-as-a-Judge for code evaluation [39] and evaluator adversarial robustness [40], positional bias [41], and fairness [42]. MEDAL [37] to our setting since it generates is closest multilingual multi-turn dialogues using multi-agent pipeline and automates labeling with GPT-4.1, followed by filtering with human curation for the final benchmark. Table 1 compares RANKJUDGE with prior benchmarks across several axes: ours is the first automated pipeline for generating multi-turn judge benchmark that is grounded in external documents, conditioned on user behavior, and built by injecting controlled error types. Table 1: Comparison of LLM judge meta evaluation benchmarks. Turn: = single turn, = multi turn. Drv. = derived meta evaluation target: means without new human preference labeling, means some manual curation and means completely dependent on human judgments. Usr. = controlled user behavior. Doc. = document grounding. Diag. = diagnostic labels beyond winner or score. Pipe. = automated benchmark generation where means partially. Eval.: Acc. = item level accuracy or agreement; Disc. = perturbation discernment; Rank = judge or system ranking.

(1) 3.1 Benchmark Construction Semi-Automated Assistant Failure Type Discovery. We construct our taxonomy of assistant behavior types through semi-automated discovery procedure. We first seed an initial set of behavior categories that are commonly observed in multi-turn conversations, drawing from and organizing prior works [3, 4, 43] on dialogue evaluation and assistant failure modes. To assess coverage, we then prompt Gemini-3.1-Pro [44] with samples from MT-Bench [1] and MT-Bench 101 [38], asking the model to verify whether each instance is captured by the existing taxonomy and, if not, to propose new assistant error types grounded in the observed failure. We scope the taxonomy to failures characteristic of multi-turn assistant behavior; coarse singleturn categories such as factual error are excluded as standalone types [45], since their multi-turn manifestations are already absorbed by more specific types. For instance, an assistant that asserts fact in turn 2 and contradicts it in turn 5 is captured by self_contradiction rather than generic hallucination, and an assistant that drops user-specified constraint after several turns is captured by instruction_forgetting. Additionally, each type targets failures that are plausible for capable assistant yet difficult to spot by surface inspection, requirement we make explicit in every flaw description so that the resulting probes stress, rather than merely confirm, the discriminative ability of strong LLM judges. Furthermore, categories are designed to have non-overlapping decision boundaries so that judges can unambiguously classify the failure type. Candidate failure types that do have overlap are merged into an existing type or dropped. Failure type discovery was supervised by the authors, who reviewed each candidate type before admission and adjudicated borderline cases and overlaps. condensed view of the resulting taxonomy is presented in Table 2, with the full set of types and definitions deferred to Appendix B.4, and the exact prompt used to elicit new error types from Gemini-3.1-Pro provided in Appendix B.3. Dual Conditioned Conversation Pair Generation. Each conversation pair is sampled under two independent conditions: an assistant failure type from the seven options in Table 2, as well as user behavior type. The assistant failure axis fixes the ground truth: by sampling target type and instructing the generator to inject one error of that type, we unambiguously define the worse conversation of the pair, the turn in which the failure occurs, and the failure category. The user 4 behavior axis adds diversity, since different user types surface different slices of the reference material and create different turn-to-turn dynamics as seen in real multi-turn use. Behaviours span seven archetypes: focused, integrative, scattered, skeptical, misinformed, exploratory, and underspecified; each represents style prompt which the user is conditioned on during generation, as defined in Appendix B.5. Each pair of better and worse conversations is produced by two separate sets of generation calls that share reference documents. Past works have created negative examples by simply injecting errors into existing text [46, 30, 39, 47]. However, when comparing two alternate conversations, if the injected error is the only change, the judge can isolate this difference rather than making complete assessment of quality. Independently sampling two conversations is also insufficient; if only the bad version faced situations where the target failure could surface, judges could again shortcut holistic comparison by pattern-matching on question types. Therefore, when generating the better conversation, we actively stage the conditions under which the selected flaw would be relevant, but condition on benign behaviour  (Table 2)  , which describes the correct way to handle the conversational pressure. For instance when the failure type is fabricated_answer, the user in the better conversation still asks an out-of-scope question, but the assistant is instructed to explicitly state the limits of its knowledge. Paired conversations are thus structurally matched on topic and conversational dynamics, differing only in handling. Both sets of generation calls follow turn-by-turn blueprint. Each turn specifies what the users question should be about, and the chunk of the reference document the assistant will need to draw on. The blueprint for the worse conversation additionally commits to bad_round_index and sketch of how the selected flaw should manifest. To keep the comparison non-trivial, the blueprint imposes requirements that the flawed turn must maintain the same tone and length as other turns, while lexical announcements of the kind stepping outside the scope for moment are disallowed. We remove ordering bias by randomizing which conversation in the pair (A/B) is assigned as worse. The full generation prompts are provided in Appendix B.6. Sample failure type , user behavior, reference documents, verdict {A, B}, flawed turn {1, . . . , R} Algorithm 1 Data generation and verification 1: for each of Ncand candidate samples do 2: Automated Quality Control. synthetic pair is only useful to the benchmark if the targeted weakness actually surfaces, appears only in the declared turn, and the rest of the content is free of clear failures. To make these judgments, we rely on the well-documented asymmetry that verification is substantially easier than generation [9, 48, 49]. We use three-layer verification cascade run by an external verifier model over every candidate pair, adding the pair to the benchmark only if it passes all three layers. Each layer is strictly discriminative where the verifier is given the intended labels (y, r, t) and the reference documents. Note that the verifier solves an easier subproblem than the judges we aim to evaluate with the benchmark, since the verifier is conditioned on the ground truth. Generate paired blueprints conditioned on the sampled inputs If coherence check of the blueprints fails, skip Generate paired conversations (CA, CB ) from the blueprints If adherence check of (CA, CB ) to blueprints fails, skip If grounding check of all atomic claims fails, skip Retain (CA, CB , y, r, t) 3: 4: 5: 6: 7: 8: 9: end for The three layers of verification check coherence, adherence, and grounding. The coherence check tests the sampled blueprint by comparing the per-turn outline of user intent, assistant focus, and the chosen failure turn against the intended ground truth and reference material. This check flags genuine semantic conflicts, e.g., blueprint that is inconsistent with the chosen user behavior or failure location. In the adherence check, conversations are examined to ensure both the user and assistant follow the blueprint globally. The better conversation must display benign behaviour across all turns, and the worse conversation must exhibit the targeted flaw in exactly the declared turn. conversation pair fails the adherence check if the user deviates from the specified behavior, the failure drifts to different turn, or if multiple flaws are present. Finally, in the grounding check the verifier extracts every atomic factual claim from each assistants turn and labels each claim as grounded or not based on the reference context. pair passes only if every turn (other than in the worse conversation) is fully grounded, ensuring that the only unsupported claim is the targeted flaw. Per-layer verification pass rates and overall retention across the three knowledge domains are reported in Table 3. The full verification prompts are provided in Appendix B.7. 5 Joint Ranking of Judges and Conversation Pairs 3.2 Let = {(jt, qt, ct)}N t=1 be the judgment results over judges = {1, . . . , } across conversation pairs = {1, . . . , Q}, with ct {0, 1} marking whether the judge correctly identified the joint criterion (y, r, t). Rather than simply report accuracies over fixed test set, we use to rate judges for two reasons. First, arena-style ratings [2] are relative to population and tolerate partial observability of the judgment results, which enables leaderboard construction without requiring full judge-pair coverage. Second, rating judges and conversation pairs jointly assigns each pair calibrated difficulty rating relative to the set of judges. This lets us dynamically curate the benchmark by difficulty. Specifically, our published evaluation slice in Section 3.3 drops the top tail of most difficult pairs because our human audit of conversation quality found this segment to have the most label noise, see Figure 2. Two rating algorithms are compatible with our bipartite framing: BradleyTerry (BT) rating, used by LM Arena [2] for LLM-vs-LLM matchups, and Empirical Interaction Propagation (EIP) [8], which is PageRank-style random walk on the correctness graph. However, EIP requires full observability of D, so we adopt BT as our default and use EIP only as cross-check. Under BT, each judge and each pair is assigned positive strength θi > 0, Q. The probability that judge produces correct verdict on pair is Pr(c = 1 j, q) = θj/(θj + θq), fit by maximum-likelihood estimation using the minorizationmaximization iteration with mean-normalization [50], and mapped to Elo via the standard 400 log10 transform. Under full observability of D, where every judge is scored on every pair, the BT ordering of judges agrees with their raw win count. BT adds two properties EIP lacks: closed-form cluster-robust confidence intervals, and stability under partial observability. Full equations, convergence criteria, and cluster-robust pair-level standard errors are deferred to Appendix B.1, with similar details for EIP in Appendix B.2. pair on which every judge produces the same verdict carries zero information about relative judge ability. Under the BT likelihood it contributes constant regardless of the ratings being fit. We therefore drop any pair whose verdicts are unanimous across the current judge set. The unanimouslyincorrect pairs are additionally likely to be mislabelled and would in any case be removed by the top-Elo curation step introduced next. 3.3 Benchmark Curation Our benchmark is completely synthetic and requires no human annotation during construction, which lets us cover the taxonomy of Section 3.1, regenerate the pool when the verifier or generator is upgraded, and scale the benchmark without proportional annotation budget. The resulting data can still be noisy, but noise is not unique to synthetic benchmarks. Widely used benchmarks carry non-trivial fraction of mislabeled or ambiguous items; MMLU [5] has roughly 6% erroneous questions in heavily evaluated subsets [7] and audits of the ten most-cited vision benchmarks find an average test-set error rate near 3% [6]. This puts curation in tension with discrimination. The hardest pairs are the most informative for separating out stronger judges, but they are the most likely to carry label noise or be ambiguous. We resolve this tradeoff conservatively: the published evaluation slice drops the top 5% of pairs by BT Elo (column Top 5% of Table 3; per-axis breakdowns in Tables 5 and 6). BT pair difficulty is judge-set-aware, so the cut is recomputed whenever the judge set changes. Two analyses support this cut: human audit and downstream SFT experiment, both of which indicate that the top-Elo slice is disproportionately likely to contain label noise, see Figure 2. Human Verification. The audit described here sits outside the benchmark construction loop. The benchmark is built end-to-end without manual curation, while this audit confirms the quality of the automated verification and justifies the design choice of dropping the top-Elo slice. The authors manually labeled uniform random sample of 120 of the 703 informative pairs as clean, ambiguous, or noise. Noise indicated that the conversation pair did not strictly match the intended ground truth (y, r, t), for example because more than one flaw was present. Ambiguous indicated that the assistant failure could not unambiguously be assigned single type in the taxonomy, but all other details were consistent. Unambiguous and correctly labeled conversation pairs were considered clean. The overall noise rate is low. Figure 2 (left) plots the cumulative fraction of audit labels across pairs with BT-Elo at most x, where we find the noise and ambiguous bands rise only in the right tail. Given the labeling effort already conducted, we do drop the 17 problematic pairs from the published slice (column Human of Table 3), but the curation pipeline does not depend on this step. The full annotation procedure, and the pair visualization tool we used are in Appendix C. 6 Figure 2: (Left) Cumulative fraction of samples annotated by humans as having ambiguous or noisy labels among conversation pairs with BT-Elo at most x. Both problematic types concentrate at the top of the difficulty distribution while easier pairs are almost entirely unambiguous. (Right) Fine-tuning Qwen3.5-4B on the top-20% slice underperforms training on the cleaner slices. Table 3: Verification and filtering cascade, split by domain. The first three filters represent automated quality verification during pair generation. The last three are applied at scoring time: Uninformative drops uninformative pairs where every judge gave the same verdict; Human drops pairs that human annotators flagged as ambiguous or noisy. The pipeline itself is fully automated and does not depend on human annotation; we apply this layer opportunistically to the 120-pair subset that was annotated for other analyses, since the flagged pairs can be excluded at no additional cost once those labels exist. Top 5% drops the top 5% of pairs by Elo, which are more likely to contain noisy labels. We split the informative pairs not used in the human audit into three BT Elo bands (top 20%, 2040%, and the remaining 60%), sample an equal number of pairs from each, and fine-tune Qwen3.5-4B [51] with LoRA [52] under an identical recipe per band (full hyperparameters in Appendix B.9). The clean pairs from the audit are held out for evaluation. Joint correctness of the fine-tuned judge improves monotonically as the training band moves down the ranking, and the bottom band achieves more than twice the score of the top band (Figure 2, right). This signal is consistent with the human audit: the top-ranked band of conversation pairs is flagged as the noisiest, leading to our decision to cut the top of the Elo distribution from the published evaluation slice.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: Experimental Settings. Our pipeline is domain-agnostic, generating benchmark of multi-turn conversation pairs through the same generation, verification, and ranking components from any corpus of knowledge-intensive context. We instantiate it on three domains, each viewable as standalone benchmark: machine learning (CS papers from RPC-Bench [53], open-access no license), biomedicine (PubMedQA [54], MIT license), and finance (S&P 500 10-K filings [55], MIT license). Per pair, the user behavior and assistant failure types are sampled independently and uniformly. All generator, verifier, and judge calls are routed through OpenRouter [56] with gpt-5.5 used for generation and verification. Every judge in our registry supports thinking, which we leave at the providers default. We exclude post-trained judges to keep the focus on inherent abilities of general-purpose LLMs, though post-training can improve performance on static problems [? 57]. RankJudge Leaderboard. Figure 3 reports BT-Elo ratings for 21 judges. The scale spans nearly 1200 Elo points from gemini-3.1-pro (1959) to gpt-oss-20b (762), implying that the benchmark separates judges across wide range of capability. gemini-3.1-pro leads with 92-point margin over the next judge gpt-5.5, while the per-domain rankings broadly preserve the order, indicating that judge skill is largely domain-invariant. The leaderboard shows trend that proprietary LLMs are still stronger than open-source models, although kimi-k2.6 and gemma-4-31b outrank several 7 Figure 3: Elo scores of 21 judges on the combined dataset. Black circles give the combined Elo with 95% CI; colored markers show per-domain Elo scores. Tick-label color denotes proprietary (blue) vs. open-source (orange) judges. Table 4 in Appendix A.1 gives detailed per-domain results. frontier models, while opus-4.7 underperforms, indicating that strong generation capability does not directly translate to strong judging. We note that gpt-5.5 is subject to strong self-preferential bias in pairwise judging, since this model was used for generation and verification. Appendix A.3 characterizes the effect by regenerating the conversation pairs with opus-4.7 as the generator; in this case gpt-5.5 drops from rank 2 to rank 7 and, opus-4.7 climbs from rank 11 to rank 3, but the rankings of the remaining 19 judges are preserved almost identically (Spearman ρ = 0.979, Kendall τ = 0.906). gemini-3.1-pro in particular ranks first under either generator despite, reflecting its genuinely superior judging ability. Elo vs. Compute. Compute cost is the other consideration for determining the best judge. Figure 4 relates each judges Elo to its mean per-match compute. The token-axis frontier collapses to three judges, opus-4.7 (549 tokens, 1510 Elo), gpt-5.5 (934 tokens, 1867), and gemini-3.1-pro (1723 tokens, 1959); long tail of mid-Elo judges spends 710k tokens per match while sitting well off the frontier, so verbosity alone does not buy ranking accuracy. The cost-axis frontier tells different story, with open-weights judges holding most of the curve: gemma-4-31b reaches 1695 Elo at $0.0023 per match, less than tenth the price of gemini-3.1-pro for 264 fewer Elo points, and gpt-oss-120b extends the frontier into the sub-cent regime at the bottom of the leaderboard. Only gemini-3.1-pro lies on both frontiers; gpt-5.5 is dominated on cost, so budget-bound users can reach competitive Elo at fraction of the spend via gemini-3-flash or an open-weights judge such as gemma-4-31b. Class Bias is Capability Gap. Figure 5 reports the per-judge gap between predicted and groundtruth failure type share. As judge strength declines, predictions collapse onto evasion with overprediction rising from near zero for the strongest judges to +41.6pp at the bottom of the leaderboard, leading to under-prediction of no_clarification and unnecessary_refusal, which fall to roughly 15pp and 18pp for the weakest judges. No such bias is observed at the top, where mistakes by gemini-3.1-pro remain within 2pp of zero on every class, which establishes that the seven-way failure taxonomy is internally non-overlapping. The class bias is therefore property of the weaker judges rather than of the data. With poor reasoning ability, judges default to evasion as crutch. The same collapse is reproduced in each domain (Figure 9 in Appendix A.5), and the per-judge confusion matrix (Figure 10 in Appendix A.6) corroborates this at the prediction level. Effect of Failure-type Prediction. We drop the failure-type component from the correctness criterion leaving (y, r), and refit BT-Elo. Panel (a) of Figure 6 compares judge Elo with and without the type label t, showing high correlation at Spearman ρ = 0.968 and Kendall τ = 0.895. This shows that including failure type prediction as correctness criterion widens the Elo gap between judges without reordering the leaderboard. Hence, the failure taxonomy further distinguishes judge ability by testing their ability to understand the reasons for failure. The same pattern holds within each domain (Figure 11 in Appendix A.7). Error Analysis and Mitigation. The leaderboard exposes surprising anomaly: opus-4.7, the headline model of the Claude family at the time of writing, sits below sonnet-4.6. This motivates deeper investigation, including whether prompt-level intervention can recover the gap. We repurpose the human audit of Section 3.3 for error-analysis over opus-4.7s incorrect verdicts on the clean pairs, summarize its recurring failure modes, and design four prompt revisions targeting them: 8 Figure 4: Judge Elo against per-match compute. Top: mean completion tokens per match (linear). Bottom: mean cost per match in USD (log). Markers are coloured by provider and the dashed grey step line traces the empirical Pareto frontier. Figure 5: Per-class prediction bias for each judge. Each cell gives the difference in percentage points between the judges predicted share of the assistant failure type and the ground-truth share. Red indicates over-prediction, while blue shows under-prediction. Columns are sorted by Elo. category hints (v2), explicit candidate comparison (v3), decision rules (v4), and chain-of-thought (v5), detailed in Appendix B.8. To bracket the magnitude of the gap we additionally ran opus-4.6 under the default prompt. Figure 6(b) plots accuracy against mean completion tokens per call for the four revisions and the four default-prompt baselines. opus-4.6 substantially outperforms every variant of opus-4.7 we tried, and none of the prompt revisions improves opus-4.7 to the accuracy level of opus-4.6 or sonnet-4.6, suggesting capability ceiling rather than prompting deficiency. The shift aligns with documented changes in the opus-4.7 release [58]: thinking is now an adaptive effort that the model can downscale at will, in place of 4.6s fixed budget. As result opus-4.7 commits to verdicts with too little deliberation when it could benefit from using more test-time compute. The Claude family case study suggests more general conclusion that prompt-level intervention cannot 9 Figure 6: (a) Judge Elo with and without failure-type correctness as criterion. (b) Accuracy vs. mean completion tokens for four default-prompt baselines and four opus-4.7 prompt revisions; the dashed step is the empirical Pareto frontier. (c) Rank correlation between the full-sample judge ranking and rankings recomputed on uniform subsamples. close model-capability gap in pairwise judging. Our four revisions cover the usual toolkit (more guidance, more deliberation, more structure, more thinking), and their joint failure suggests that practitioners are better served by switching judges than by tuning prompts. Robustness and Benchmark Cost. To verify the partial-observability argument of Section 3.2, we refit BT-Elo on uniform subsamples of the judgment results at fractions {10%, 20%, 50%, 80%} and report the rank correlation with the full data in Figure 6(c). Spearman ρ exceeds 0.95 from 10% onward and Kendall τ crosses the same threshold at 50%, so the leaderboard can be reproduced at fraction of the full cost with negligible distortion to rankings. The same conclusion holds within each domain (Figure 12 in Appendix A.8). The leaderboard is also stable to two further perturbations. First, refitting with the top-5% Elo conversation pairs included matches the published ordering at Spearman ρ 0.997, with the top-10 judge ranking preserved (Figure 13 in Appendix A.9), confirming that the BT rating is robust under residual noise. Second, swapping BT-Elo for the difficulty-aware EIP ranking leaves the ordering nearly unchanged (ρ 0.94, Appendix A.10). We adopt BT as the primary score because it yields closed-form cluster-robust error bars and leaderboard that stays stable under partial observability. Pairwise versus Pointwise. RANKJUDGE can also be run with the judges scoring conversations pointwise on 110 Likert scale rather than choosing between candidates. The two protocols agree at coarse level (Spearman ρ = 0.809) but the judge rankings diverge because these approaches measure different capabilities. Pairwise tests discrimination between two candidate conversations, while pointwise tests absolute calibration of conversation quality without reference. We report this comparison in detail in Appendix A.11.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: We presented RANKJUDGE, benchmark generator for evaluating LLM-as-a-judge on multi-turn, reference-grounded conversations. By constructing each pair to have an unambiguously worse conversation while keeping the subject material similarly challenging, but not identical, the pipeline supports strict joint correctness criterion that separates correct judgments from correct guesses. Three-layer automated verification combined with dynamic BradleyTerry Elo-based curation keeps construction fully synthetic without sacrificing label quality. The verification step and label quality was cross-checked by human audit and held-out fine-tuning experiment. Across 21 frontier judges and three subject matter domains, the resulting leaderboard spans nearly 1200 Elo points, surfaces several open-weight checkpoints that outrank proprietary models, and exposes sharp failure type bias in weaker judges. The judge ranking is stable across different curation setups, swapping the BradleyTerry rating system for the EIP system, and under subsampling which reduces the cost of benchmarking.

sections.title: A Additional Results
sections.content: A.1 Detailed Judge Elo Ratings Table 4 gives each judges combined Elo together with the per-domain breakdown, where confidence intervals are obtained from the cluster-robust sandwich estimator of Appendix B.1. The intervals tighten toward the middle of the leaderboard, where contested matches concentrate, and widen at the extremes, where the strongest and weakest judges win or lose decisively against most opponents and so accumulate little discriminative information. Finance is the lowest-Elo domain for most mid-leaderboard judges, consistent with the heavier numerical reasoning load imposed by 10-K filings; gpt-5.4, the only judge whose Finance Elo exceeds its ML and Biomedical scores, is notable exception. Table 4: Elo of LLM judges (95% CI). Best per column in bold. Sorted by Elo measured by the combined dataset (All).

Open Open Open Open Open A.2 Problem Rankings Assistant failure type separates pair difficulty cleanly. no_clarification (median Elo 1525) and disorganized (1475) consistently beat judges, while unnecessary_refusal and fabricated_answer (both 849) are the easiest to catch: refusals stand out in context and fabrications are directly checkable against the grounding document. User behavior, by contrast, barely shifts pair difficulty: the seven categories span only 979 to 1247 in median Elo with heavily overlapping IQRs. This is by design. The user behavior axis is included to diversify the conversational contexts in the benchmark rather than to differentiate judges, so the lack of separation here is the expected outcome. A.3 Model Bias and Self-Preference Setup. To isolate the effect of the generator we re-ran the pair-generation pipeline second time with opus-4.7 substituted for gpt-5.5 as the generator and the rest of the recipe held fixed: the same source documents, the dual-conditioning prompts of Appendix B.6, the coherence, adherence, and grounding gates of Appendix B.7, and the same 21-judge panel evaluated under the pairwise prompt of Appendix B.8. To bound API cost the parallel set was restricted to 50 items per domain, Figure 7: Per-pair Elo on the combined dataset, grouped by (a) assistant failure type and (b) user behavior type. Categories are sorted by median Elo; violins show the full distribution, overlaid boxes mark the interquartile range (IQR) with the median in red, and jittered points mark individual pairs. Figure 8: Same-model preference does not distort the rest of the leaderboard. Each panel is slope chart of judge Elo ranks under the published pair set generated by gpt-5.5 (left axis) against the parallel set generated by opus-4.7 (right axis), with ranks recomputed from BradleyTerry fit within the panels judge subset. 17 drawn from the same source pool used for the published evaluation, yielding 150 candidate pairs of which 131 survive verification. BradleyTerry is fit independently on each conditions match set with the MM iteration of Appendix B.1: on the published top_removed slice (652 pairs, after dropping the ambiguous and noise pairs and the top-Elo trim), and on the filtered slice (107 non-trivial pairs). Since the parallel set has no human audit it cannot be cut to the clean subset. Spearman ρ and Kendall τ in each panel are computed on judge ranks computed within that panels subset, so they are invariant to the absolute Elo scale and to which judges are removed. Findings. As shown in Figure 8, panel (a) keeps all 21 judges and exposes clear self-preference: gpt-5.5 slips from rank 2 to rank 7 once it is no longer the generator, and opus-4.7 climbs from rank 11 to rank 3 once it is. The other 19 judges, whose outputs are never used to construct pairs, are nevertheless ordered almost identically across the two conditions. gemini-3.1-pro is in particular ranked first under both generators, even though no condition uses its outputs, suggesting that its lead is driven by judging ability rather than generator alignment. Panel (b) drops the two generators and recomputes the ranks on the same matches: Spearman ρ rises from 0.917 to 0.979 and Kendall τ from 0.810 to 0.906. The bias is therefore localized to whichever model is producing the conversations; one generator still has to be chosen for the published evaluation, but that choice does not materially shift any other judges ranking. A.4 Filtering Cascade Tables 3, 5, and 6 report the filtering cascade sliced three ways: by domain, by assistant failure type, and by user behavior type. Of 1200 generated pairs, 652 survive every layer (overall survival 54.3%). The dominant signal sits on the assistant-failure axis. unnecessary_refusal and fabricated_answer clear the adherence verifier with zero loss, while disorganized loses 130 of its 174 generated pairs at that single layer and ends up the rarest type in the published evaluation set (30 pairs, 17.2% survival). The per-domain and per-user-behavior survival rates are far tighter and have no comparable bottleneck (48.560.0% across domains, 46.461.8% across user behaviors), so the shape of the cascade is set almost entirely by which flaw is being injected. The next paragraph and worked example explain why disorganized is the hard case: it is the only assistant flaw whose detection condition is structural rather than categorical, so turn must lack logical flow strongly enough for the verifier to confirm the flaw, but not so strongly that every judge picks the better conversation on sight. Table 5: Verification and filtering cascade, split by assistant failure type. Refusals, fabrications, contradictions, and instruction violations are binary properties of turns content: turn either declines an answerable question or it does not. disorganized is different. The flaw lives in the ordering and foregrounding of correct content, not in the content itself, so the bad turn must reshuffle the same facts that the good turn presents cleanly. Make the reshuffle too mild and the adherence verifier rejects the pair, with rejection reasons like Round 2 is mostly clear and foregrounds the main correction, so it does not actually exhibit the required disorganized flaw.2 Make the reshuffle too aggressive and the bad turn 2Verbatim from the adherence.bad_issue field of rejected disorganized pair in verification.json; analogous strings (clear and logically organized, gives clear lead answer) account for the bulk of the 130-pair attrition in row disorganized of Table 5. 18 Table 6: Verification and filtering cascade, split by user behavior type. The example below is from pair id=40589ed7f7f0 (ML, user_behavior_type=misinformed, assistant_behavior_type=disorganized, bad_round_index=2). The users second turn pushes misinformed claim that the papers main novelty was DP-Adam beating DP-SGD, that no DP-SGD baseline was reported, and that prior work had privately fine-tuned GPT-2 with DP-SGD. Both assistant responses contain the same three corrections: the E2E DP-SGD BLEU number (63.17), that DP-Adam was not claimed as the contribution, and that the cited GPT-2 prior work fine-tuned GPT-2 non-privately. The only difference is structural: convo_a leads with the answer to the users question (DP-Adam was not claimed as new algorithm), while convo_b opens with peripheral hyperparameter detail and threads the lead correction through privacy-by-post-processing aside, leaving it half paragraph in. Clean DP-Adam was not claimed as new algorithm; it was already common in DP libraries, and it uses the same gradient privatization mechanism as DPSGD before the Adam update. The papers contribution was showing that full fine-tuning of large pretrained language models can work well under DP when done with suitable hyperparameters, aligned objectives, and the memory trick. Also, the referenced prior work did not privately finetune GPT-2: it fine-tuned GPT-2 non-privately and used privately trained feedforward network for private experiments. Disorganized On E2E at epsilon = 8, their extra DP-SGD experiment used learning rate 3 and clipping norm 0.1 and reached BLEU 63.17 over 3 seeds, which was on par with DP-Adam. DP-Adam itself was not claimed as the contribution, even though after the same subsampled Gaussian gradient privatization, Adam-style updates preserve privacy by post-processing. The contribution was applying known DP optimization effectively to full language-model fine-tuning, with the memory trick making it computationally practical. Also, the cited GPT-2 work did not privately fine-tune GPT-2 with DP-SGD; it fine-tuned GPT-2 nonprivately. . . The two responses are factually equivalent against the source; only the ordering differs. This pair sits in the narrow band where the verifier accepts (the lead answer is genuinely buried, mid-paragraph, after hyperparameter aside and post-processing detour) and judges still split (the correct facts are all present, so judge has to actually read for structure rather than skim for missing content). Most generation attempts miss this band in one direction or the other, which is why disorganized loses three quarters of its candidate pairs at the adherence layer alone. A.5 Class Bias by Domain Figure 9 shows that the prediction-bias signature is essentially domain-invariant: across Machine Learning, Biomedical, and Finance, the same column pattern repeats, with evasion the dominant red column and no_clarification the dominant blue one, and the magnitude of the bias growing as Elo decreases from left to right. The other five categories carry only small, noisy biases. This mirrors the confusion structure of Appendix A.6: weak judges fold ambiguous failures into evasion and rarely commit to no_clarification, regardless of the underlying domain. 19 (a) Machine Learning (b) Biomedical (c) Finance Figure 9: Per-class prediction bias for each judge across the three domains. Each cell gives the difference between the judges predicted share of the assistant failure type and its ground-truth share, in percentage points; red indicates over-prediction and blue under-prediction. Columns are sorted by Elo. 20 A.6 Assistant Failure Type Confusion Figure 10: Per-judge confusion of assistant failure type predictions, row-normalized so each the four lowrow sums to one. est. Class labels abbreviate the seven assistant failure types (SC self_contradiction, EV evasion, DO disorganized, FA fabricated_answer, IF instruction_forgetting, NC no_clarification, UR unnecessary_refusal). the four highest-Elo judges; bottom row: Top row: Figure 10 contrasts how the four highestand lowest-Elo judges classify the assistant failure type. The top row concentrates nearly all of its mass on the diagonal across most categories; the only residual confusion is on disorganized and no_clarification, both of which leak modestly into evasion. The bottom row collapses toward single dump bin: weak judges relabel large share of no_clarification, self_contradiction, disorganized, and unnecessary_refusal predictions as evasion, suggesting that taxonomy resolution, not failure detection, is what separates the leaderboard tail from its head. A.7 Failure-type Correctness by Domain Figure 11: Per-domain version of Figure 6(a). Judge Elo with the full correctness criterion (x-axis) vs. Elo with the failure-type label dropped from the loss (y-axis), refit independently within each domain. Each marker is judge; the dashed line marks = and the red line is the least-squares fit. Per-panel Spearman ρ and Kendall τ are computed on the underlying Elo scores. Figure 11 repeats the with-vs.-without-type analysis inside each domain. Spearman ρ ranges from 0.969 (ML) to 0.993 (Finance) and Kendall τ from 0.885 to 0.959, with the red fits sitting below the 21 diagonal at the top of every panel. The seven-way taxonomy therefore widens the Elo range within every domain without reordering the local leaderboard, matching the combined-dataset result. A.8 Subsample Stability by Domain Figure 12: Per-domain version of Figure 6(c). Each panel reports Spearman ρ and Kendall τ between the full-sample top_removed BT ranking and the partial_obs BT ranking refit on uniform subsamples at fractions {10%, 20%, 50%, 80%}, computed independently within each domain. Figure 12 reproduces the subsample-stability check within each domain. Spearman ρ exceeds 0.98 from the 50% subsample onward in all three domains and Kendall τ crosses 0.9 for Machine Learning and Finance at the same fraction. Biomedical is the slowest to stabilise (ρ = 0.786 at 10%), consistent with the tighter mid-leaderboard band for that domain reported in Appendix A.1, but joins the other two by the 50% fraction. The match-cost reduction therefore transfers to per-domain leaderboards as well, with marginally larger minimum sample required for Biomedical. A.9 Top-Ranked Pair Removal The published evaluation pipeline applies single noise-mitigation step on top of automatic verification: the top-ranked pair removal, which drops the highest-Elo pairs from the filtered pool. The human audit of Appendix and the SFT-with-noise experiments of Appendix B.9 are not pipeline components but supporting evidence for this step, showing that the top-Elo cohort is where mislabelled ambiguous and noise pairs concentrate, so removing it strips the bulk of residual label noise. Figure 13 closes the loop: refitting Elo on the raw filtered slice yields leaderboard nearly identical to the published one, with Spearman ρ 0.997, Kendall τ 0.981, and the top-10 set preserved in full on all four slices. We read this two ways. First, the automated quality control of Appendix B.7 already removes most of the label noise, so the additional top-Elo trim and the human audit only have small residual noise left to clean up. Second, the BradleyTerry ranking and the surrounding evaluation pipeline are robust under that small residual noise. A.10 Ranking Methods: BT vs. EIP Figure 14 compares the BradleyTerry Elo leaderboard with the difficulty-aware EIP ranking of Appendix B.2, on the combined dataset and each domain. The two methods agree strongly across all four slices: Spearman ρ ranges from 0.944 to 0.953 and Kendall τ from 0.820 to 0.852, with the top-3 set always sharing two of three judges and the top-10 sets overlapping by 80% to 90%. EIP is designed to up-weight judges that solve pairs which fool strong judges, so its near-identical ordering with Elo indicates that the benchmark does not contain pocket of hard pairs whose signal Elo discards. We adopt BradleyTerry Elo as the primary score throughout the paper for its closedform cluster-robust confidence intervals (Appendix B.1) and its stability under partial observability (Figure 12). A.11 Pointwise Judges Setup. The pointwise prompt of Appendix B.8 differs from the pairwise default in that each judge sees single conversation in isolation and returns 1-10 Likert score together with an is_flawed flag, the worst-round index, and the A-G flaw category. Pairwise verdicts are derived post-hoc by 22 Figure 13: Judge ranks with (x-axis) and without (y-axis) the top-ranked pair removal, one panel per slice. The with condition is the published top_removed slice; the without condition is the raw filtered slice taken straight from the automatic verification pipeline of Appendix B.7. Each marker is judge; the dashed line is = x. Both axes are inverted so that rank 1 sits at the top-right. Per-panel Spearman ρ and Kendall τ are computed on the underlying Elo scores. scoring convo_a and convo_b independently and taking the higher-scoring side as the winner; equal scores are recorded as ties and given 0.5/0.5 credit so the same BradleyTerry machinery applies. The experiment is run on 100-pair sample stratified by domain and assistant failure type, judged by the same 21-model panel; the pairwise axis in Figure 15(a) is recomputed on the identical sample, so the only difference between the two axes is the prompt mode. Findings. Pointwise and pairwise rankings agree at the coarse level (Spearman ρ = 0.809, Kendall τ = 0.660) but the judge rankings differ. This divergence is expected, because the two protocols measure different judge capabilities: pairwise tests discrimination between two candidate conversations under direct comparison, while pointwise tests absolute calibration of conversation quality without reference. Panel (b) makes the calibration axis explicit: the mean score gap between the good and flawed conversations exceeds 4 Likert points for gemini-3.1-pro but falls below 1 for the weakest three judges, per-judge resolution that pairwise discards once scores collapse to binary winner. Pairwise remains the more discriminative protocol for ranking judges; the pointwise score-gap serves as complementary diagnostic for absolute calibration. 23 Figure 14: Judge ranks under BradleyTerry Elo (x-axis) and Empirical Interaction Propagation (y-axis), one panel per slice. Each marker is judge; the dashed line is = x. Both axes are inverted so that rank 1 sits at the top-right. Per-panel Spearman ρ and Kendall τ are computed on the underlying scores. 24 Figure 15: Pointwise vs. pairwise judging on 100-pair stratified sample. (a) Each judges pointwise Elo plotted against its pairwise Elo recomputed on the same pair set; the dashed line marks = x. (b) Per-judge pointwise score-gap: the mean Likert score on the good conversation in each pair minus the mean score on the flawed one, sorted descending.

B.3 Prompt for Assistant Failure Type Discovery The following box gives the prompt used for automated discovery and refinement of the assistant failure type taxonomy used in Section 3.1. 27 Assistant Failure Type Discovery Prompt You are an expert in multi-turn conversation evaluation, specializing in identifying the challenging scenarios, conducting failure mode and taxonomy analysis. Your task is fourfold: (1) Task 1: per-sample annotation. Apply an existing taxonomy to label assistant and user behaviors in multi-turn conversations (2) Task 2: identify taxonomy gaps. Identify cases that do NOT fit the taxonomy (3) Task 3: taxonomy refinement. Categorize the missed cases and propose refined or new categories grounded in evidence (4) Task 4: insights generation. Produce outputs for downstream analysis, explanations for the proposal. ## TAXONOMY DEFINITIONS ASSISTANT_BEHAVIORS = { "disorganized": {...}, "fabricated_answer": "unnecessary_refusal": ... } {...}, {...}, USER_BEHAVIORS = { "focused": "...", "skeptical": "...", "misinformed": "...", "exploratory": "...", ... } ## GENERAL GUIDELINES - Be precise, not verbose; avoid generic explanations. - Ground reasoning in observed data; do NOT hallucinate missing context. - Prefer concrete behavioral descriptions over abstract wording. Identify ambiguities in definitions. - Be critical of the taxonomy when necessary, but only propose changes supported by repeated evidence. ## DATA EXAMPLES B.4 Assistant Failure Types The following boxes define the expected behaviours for assistant failure types, and the corresponding benign behaviours when no failure is present. These are the full versions of the behaviour specifications that assistants are conditioned on, corresponding to Table 2. self_contradiction Flaw The assistant subtly contradicts claim from an earlier correct turn not by lexically flipping it, but by shifting qualifier (primary factor becomes one of several factors), reframing emphasis, or stating something whose implications conflict with what the assistant said before. Use different wording so the inconsistency isnt surface-detectable; catching it requires mentally reconciling the flawed later turn against an earlier one. The flawed round is the later turn whose claim no longer matches what was said before, while the earlier reference turn remains accurate and grounded in the source. Avoid lexically obvious flips, and do not restate the earlier claim verbatim immediately before contradicting it. Non-flawed turns stay consistent with the assistants own prior statements. 28 self_contradiction Benign Stay consistent with your own earlier turns match the scope, strength, and qualifiers of prior claims across the conversation. If your understanding genuinely evolves, explicitly acknowledge what changed and why rather than silently shifting. evasion Flaw The chatbot produces response that looks thorough and knowledgeable but avoids giving the user what they actually asked for. This can take two forms, or blend them: (a) surface specificity that hedges on the central claim citing number for minor result while glossing the key finding, or naming method without explaining what makes it distinct; (b) tangential drift emphasizing adjacent or related material so the answer reads plausibly while missing the core point. Either way, the response should look reasonable to skimming reader but leave the user unable to verify or act on the central claim. Non-flawed turns answer the users actual question directly with the key details, exact numbers, and specific terms the question demands. evasion Benign For each question, provide complete, specific, on-topic answer every sentence directly addresses what the user asked, with the key details, exact numbers, and specific terms that are relevant to the actual question. disorganized Flaw The chatbot buries key information in text that looks similar in length and formatting to clean responses but lacks logical flow jumping between points, mixing high-level claims with low-level details mid-paragraph, and failing to foreground the answer the user actually asked for. The flaw is structural, not visual: the response should not look like wall of text. The correct content is present but requires significant effort to extract. Non-flawed turns should present information clearly and directly. disorganized Benign Present information with clear structure lead with the key point, organize logically, make the answer easy to scan and extract insights from. fabricated_answer Flaw The chatbot fabricates confident-sounding answer when the question is not answerable from the reference material, instead of honestly saying it doesnt have enough information. Nonflawed turns should acknowledge limitations when the question goes beyond what the reference material supports. fabricated_answer Benign Some of the users questions go beyond what the reference material covers. The assistant recognizes these and honestly says it doesnt have enough information rather than guessing, while answering confidently when the material does cover the question. Demonstrate clear calibration between what the source supports and what it doesnt at least one turn should acknowledge limit. instruction_forgetting Flaw Early in the conversation the user establishes an explicit content-level directive scope restriction (focus only on the ablation results, skip the architecture discussion; only pull from the abstract, not the full methods), an audience/framing (Im clinician making treatment decisions, not student; graduate-level CS, assume transformer basics), self-context (Im researching Alzheimers specifically, not dementia broadly; Ive already read section 3, dont re-explain it), prioritization directive (I care more about limitations than strengths; methodology over results), or negative content instruction (dont walk me through the reasoning, just give the conclusion; dont re-cover material we already discussed). The assistant honors the directive through the intermediate rounds. In the flawed round it silently violates the directive while otherwise answering competently pulling in out-of-scope material to provide context, drifting to different audience level, re-explaining what the user said to skip, or slipping into the deprioritized angle. The violation must be partial or plausibly-justified (not wholesale abandonment) and must not be lexically announced no let me expand beyond. . . , stepping outside the scope for moment. . . . Do not use format/stylistic directives (bullets vs prose, word counts, citation style) since those are surface-detectable and dont test reasoning. Non-flawed turns apply the directive consistently. instruction_forgetting Benign Early in the conversation the user establishes an explicit content-level directive scope restriction, audience/framing, self-context, prioritization, or negative-content instruction (not surface formatting). The assistant tracks the directive and applies it consistently across every subsequent turn, even when not restated. Responses should demonstrate that the directive is actively shaping what gets included, emphasized, or omitted not by naming the directive, but through the content choices themselves. If later question would genuinely conflict with the directive, flag the conflict and ask rather than silently deviating. no_clarification Flaw When the users question is ambiguous or underspecified e.g., it could refer to multiple parts of the material, or lacks enough context to give precise answer the chatbot picks one interpretation and answers confidently instead of asking the user to clarify. Non-flawed turns should ask for clarification when the question is genuinely ambiguous. no_clarification Benign At least one of the users questions is genuinely ambiguous or underspecified (could refer to multiple parts of the material, or lacks enough context for precise answer). On those, the assistant asks for clarification before answering rather than silently assuming one interpretation. On unambiguous questions, the assistant answers directly without performative clarification requests. unnecessary_refusal Flaw The chatbot unnecessarily refuses or hedges on questions that ARE answerable from the reference material, saying things like dont have enough information or cant determine that when the answer is clearly available. Non-flawed turns should answer confidently using the reference material. unnecessary_refusal Benign The user asks questions that sit near the edge of what the source covers but are answerable if the assistant reads the material carefully. The assistant answers confidently using the available 30 information without unnecessary hedging or refusal. Only hedge when question is genuinely outside the sources scope. B.5 User Behavior Types The following boxes define the user-behavior taxonomy used in Section 3.1. Each generated pair samples one user behavior, which conditions how the simulated user interacts with the assistant across all rounds. The seven behaviors split into navigation patterns (how the user moves through the material) and epistemic stances (how the user relates to the materials content). Keys match the labels used by the pipeline. focused The user picks one or two specific aspects of the material and explores them in depth across multiple turns, asking follow-up questions that build on previous answers. integrative The user asks broad questions connecting different parts of the material - linking details across sections, comparing information, wanting synthesis rather than isolated facts. scattered The user jumps abruptly between unrelated aspects of the material without transitions, forcing the assistant to handle rapid context switches. skeptical The user plays devils advocate - questioning claims, asking for evidence, pointing out apparent tensions between answers, and requiring justification before accepting conclusions. misinformed The user confidently states incorrect things about the material (wrong numbers, misattributed claims, flipped conclusions) and builds questions on these false premises. The assistant should correct these errors. exploratory The user treats the material as starting point and asks about implications, broader connections, or extensions that may go beyond what the reference material covers. underspecified The user sometimes asks vague or ambiguous questions - using unclear references, omitting key qualifiers, or phrasing questions that could apply to multiple parts of the material - forcing the assistant to either guess the intent or ask for clarification. B.6 Prompts for Dual Conditioned Conversation Pair Generation The following boxes display the prompts used for generating conversation based on blueprints, as described in Section 3.1. The two prompts give instructions for generating good conversation, and worse conversation which intentionally has weaknesses. 31 good_convo This conversation will be used in pairwise comparison against counterpart conversation that contains subtle "{flaw_name}" flaw in exactly one round. The goal of the pair is to test whether an LLM judge can pick the better conversation --- which only works if your conversation is absolutely correct on every dimension and naturally embodies the paired virtue, without performing it loudly. If your conversation is obviously stronger than clean conversation with one subtle flaw, the test becomes trivial. Aim to read as ordinary high quality, not as demonstration of the virtue. Given this reference document, generate natural multi-turn conversation ({n} rounds, each round = 1 user + 1 assistant message). The context is reference material - it defines what the assistant knows about the topic. The conversation does NOT need to cover all of it. focused discussion exploring few topics in depth is better than superficially touching everything. First, write turn-by-turn plan with one labeled entry per round in the form Round 1: ..., Round 2: ..., ..., Round {n}: .... Every round MUST have its own entry --- do not collapse rounds into single paragraph or skip ahead. For each round, capture (a) what the user asks and what motivates it (building on the prior round where natural), and (b) what the assistants response will focus on from the reference material. The plan should form coherent arc, not isolated Q&A. Then write the conversation faithfully executing the plan. User behavior: "{user_behavior_name}" - {user_behavior_desc} Assistant virtue to embody naturally (the quality every round must quietly live up to --- do NOT overact it): {assistant_virtue} Paired flaw the counterpart conversation will contain (for your awareness only --- do NOT mention, contrast, or over-compensate against it; just never exhibit it): { assistant_flaw} User rules: - Has read the reference material. Asks questions driven by genuine curiosity, not to quiz the assistant. - Apply the above behavior consistently across ALL rounds - it should shape what the user asks, how they react, and how the conversation progresses. - NOT passive question-asker - reacts to answers, builds on them, pushes back, or redirects. - Keep messages SHORT and natural (1-3 sentences). Real users dont write essays. Assistant rules: - Every round must be clean on every dimension: accurate, grounded, specific, on-topic , well-structured, responsive, and appropriately calibrated. No flaw category should surface in any round. - Answers must be grounded in the provided context. Do NOT fabricate information beyond what the context provides. - If user question goes beyond what the context covers, honestly acknowledge the limitation rather than guessing or fabricating. - Be specific - use the actual numbers, details, and conclusions from the reference material. - Be concise and direct (2-5 sentences per response). Avoid filler, repetition, and preamble. Match the tone, length, and confidence of typical clean response --- do not inflate quality signals to appear more virtuous. === Reference Context === {context} 32 worse_convo Given this reference document, generate natural multi-turn conversation ({n} rounds, each round = 1 user + 1 assistant message) where the chatbot has specific weakness. Chatbot weakness: **{behavior_type}** Description: {behavior_desc} The context is reference material - it defines what the assistant knows about the topic. The conversation does NOT need to cover all of it. focused discussion is fine . First, write turn-by-turn plan in the "reasoning" field with one labeled entry per round in the form Round 1: ..., Round 2: ..., ..., Round {n}: .... Every round MUST have its own entry --- do not collapse the plan into single justification paragraph or skip ahead. For each round, capture (a) what the user asks and what motivates it (reacting to the prior round where natural), and (b) what the assistants response will focus on from the reference material. The plan should form coherent arc, not isolated Q&A. Pick one round to be the flawed one and, inside that rounds entry, describe concretely how the "{behavior_type}" weakness will surface there --- the clean rounds before and after should carry the conversation as if no flaw exists. Set "bad_round_index" to that round (1-indexed). Then write the conversation faithfully executing the plan. User behavior: "{user_behavior_name}" - {user_behavior_desc} User rules: - Has read the reference material. Asks questions driven by genuine curiosity. - Apply the above behavior consistently across ALL rounds. - NOT passive question-asker - reacts to answers naturally. If the chatbot gives an evasive answer, the user may follow up, push back, or move on - whatever fits. Do NOT have the user explicitly diagnose or name the flaw. - Keep messages SHORT and natural (1-3 sentences). Chatbot (assistant) rules: - Answers must be grounded in the provided context. Do NOT fabricate information beyond what the context provides. - Be specific - use the actual numbers, details, and conclusions from the reference material. - Be concise and direct (2-5 sentences per response). Avoid filler, repetition, and preamble. - In exactly 1 round, the assistant response should exhibit the "{behavior_type}" weakness. The flaw should require careful reading to detect --- reader skimming should NOT notice anything wrong. Keep the same confident tone and similar response length as other rounds. - The flawed round must exhibit ONLY the "{behavior_type}" flaw --- no other weakness category should appear in it. Every dimension of the response other than the named flaw must remain clean: accurate, grounded, specific, on-topic, well-structured, and responsive. All non-flawed rounds must be clean on every dimension. - Do NOT mention or acknowledge that responses are flawed. === Reference Context === {context} 33 B.7 Prompts for Automated Quality Control The following boxes display the prompts used for automated quality control of generated conversations, namely for checking coherence, adherence, and grounding as described in Section 3.1. Leave *_issue as "" when ok. Keep issues to one sentence. 34 adherence You are auditing whether two rendered conversations actually followed their generation plans. Read the plans and then the conversations and answer yes/no globally for each side. For the GOOD conversation: - Did the assistant consistently display the stated virtue across ALL rounds? - Did the user maintain the stated user behavior across ALL rounds? Leave *_issue as "" when ok. One sentence per issue. 35 grounding You are fact-checking an assistants responses against reference document. For each listed assistant turn, extract every factual atomic claim it makes (specific numbers, named entities, mechanisms, cause/effect statements, attributions) and mark each claim as grounded or not. Rules: - claim is grounded: true only if it is directly supported by the reference context below. - Paraphrases and reasonable summaries of supported content count as grounded. - Reasonable inferences or synthesis across multiple context statements also count as grounded, even if no single sentence states them verbatim. - Unsupported extrapolation, invented details, or claims beyond the context are grounded: false. - Ignore conversational filler, hedges, and meta-statements ("good question", "let me explain"). - Do NOT extract negative/absence claims ("X is not Y", "X doesnt mention Z") unless the context directly contradicts them - absence is not verifiable at claim granularity . - Do NOT extract meta-statements where the assistant is evaluating another statement rather than asserting fact ("it is inaccurate to claim...", "that earlier answer was wrong"). - Aim for 1-5 atomic claims per turn.
