sections.title: C Human Audit
sections.content: C.1 Participants The authors performed the audit themselves. The audit was carried out as part of regular research duties; no additional compensation was paid and no external annotators were recruited, so no IRB review was required. Each annotator was assigned disjoint bundle of 20 pairs. C.2 Annotation Tool The pair-audit interface is Streamlit application that surfaces every artefact relevant to single pair on one screen. The sidebar exposes filters for domain, declared assistant behavior, and turn count, together with sort modes including ascending joint correctness (suspicious first) and per-component accuracy. The main view splits into five tabs: Overview (ground-truth verdict, bad_round_index, assistant_behavior_type, user_behavior_type, and per-judge accuracy summary), Plan (plan.good and plan.bad side-by-side), Conversations (convo_a and convo_b rendered as col43 lapsible per-turn cards, with the injected-flaw turn opened by default and tagged with the declared assistant_behavior_type), Verification (per-claim grounding decisions plus the coherence and adherence gates from Section 3.1), and Judges (each of the 21 judges raw verdict, picked turn, picked behavior type, and reasoning). The annotator commits one of three labels (clean, ambiguous, or noise) together with free-text note; labels persist to per-annotator JSON file. Figure 16: The pair-audit interface used for the human label-noise audit. The annotator inspects the ground-truth metadata, the good and bad plans, the two conversation transcripts (with the injectedflaw turn opened and tagged), the three verifier outputs, and each of the 21 judges verdicts and reasoning, then commits one of three labels (clean, ambiguous, noise) along with free-text note. The screenshot shows the Conversations tab on representative ML pair. C.3 Annotation Procedure Auditing every pair end-to-end is impractical, so each annotator works only through the candidate noisy slice surfaced by Section 3.3 (20 pairs across two parts). Each pair is processed using the following five-step procedure, and the disposition rubric at the end of the subsection maps the resulting observations to one of clean, ambiguous, or noise. 1. Triage by judge-disagreement signal. Open the Overview tab and scan the per-judge accuracy summary. Pairs that nearly all judges miss, or that split close to 50/50 between the two candidate conversations, are the most likely to be mislabelled and are inspected first; pairs on which the ensemble agrees with the declared verdict are quickly skimmed and moved through. 2. Confirm the injected flaw is on-target. On the Plan tab, read plan.bad and verify that the planned weakness is consistent with the declared assistant_behavior_type. The plan is single narrative paragraph rather than turn-by-turn script, but it must be specific enough to determine which turn realises the flaw. Hold the planned weakness in mind for the remaining steps; if the plan describes different category of flaw than the one declared, label noise immediately. 3. Confirm the bad conversation executes the plan. On the Conversations tab, locate the turn at bad_round_index in convo_b and verify that the assistant response realises the 44 planned weakness, neither different category nor milder version. If convo_b exhibits different assistant_behavior_type than the one declared, or if no turn realises the planned flaw, label noise. 4. Cross-check using the judge ensemble. Open the Judges tab. If the strongest judges in the registry (gemini-3.1-pro, gpt-5.5, opus-4.7) consistently miss the verdict while several weaker judges (gpt-oss-20b, gemma-4-31b) get it right, treat the pair as suspicious. The strong judges reasoning text usually points at competing flaw in convo_a, which is the next thing to verify. 5. Verify the good conversation has no competing flaw. For each strong judge that picked the wrong side: (i) locate the disputed turn in convo_a on the Conversations tab, (ii) retrieve the relevant span from metadata.context (the source document), and (iii) consult an external LLM with focused fact-check prompt that contains only the disputed turn, the matching source span, and the users preceding question. Verify the LLMs answer by hand against the source. This step is the most expensive and is the rate-limiting one on MED and FIN pairs, where domain expertise is needed to resolve subtle factual disputes. The disposition rubric maps observations to labels: clean when the injected weakness is the only material flaw and both conversations are otherwise faithful to their plans and to the source; ambiguous when failure could fit more than one type defined in the taxonomy; and noise when there was any deviation from the intended label (y, r, t). Examples of noise include when the better conversation contains any clear flaw, the worse conversation has no clear flaw or flaw in the wrong turn, or when the failure type is different than t. The label-noise rates surfaced by this procedure are reported in Section A.9 and motivate the top-Elo trim of the published evaluation slice.

sections.title: D Examples
sections.content: We illustrate the joint correctness criterion on two pairs labelled clean by the audit of Section C. The first is presented in full (source context, both plans, both transcripts, and curated subset of judge responses) to make every component of the criterion visible. The second uses single 21-row table to show how the criterion partitions the full judge ensemble on representative pair. D.1 Full Example: Unnecessary Refusal in ML The pair (id = 2d63594b210e) is drawn from the ML domain, with user_behavior_type = underspecified and assistant_behavior_type = unnecessary_refusal. The injected flaw lives in the second assistant turn (bad_round_index = 2); convo_b is the better conversation (better_is_a = FALSE). Source. The reference document is the ICLR 2022 paper MCL-GAN: Generative Adversarial Networks with Multiple Specialized Discriminators. The relevant content for the second turn is the papers description of the flag variables vi,m and uj,m, which are set by the k-largest discriminator scores per sample and are explicitly not included in backpropagation; balance loss prevents collapse onto single discriminator. Plans. The full text of both plans is shown in the two boxes below. The behavioural divergence between them is highlighted in red: turn 2 is where the injected flaw lives, and turn 3 picks different follow-up topic in each plan (GMAN comparison vs. ADA trick) while keeping both planned responses clean. plan.good Turn 1. The user asks whether MCL-GAN was only validated in small or toy settings, motivated by concern about mode-collapse methods not scaling. The assistant will point to the papers three-task experimental coverage: unconditional image generation, image-toimage translation, and text-to-image synthesis, including high-resolution conditional synthesis and comparisons with strong baselines. Turn 2. Building on that, the user asks vaguely how the discriminators actually specialize and whether the flag variables are learned. The assistant will explain the MCL k-max selection rule for and flags, that they are not backpropagated through, and how the balance loss prevents collapse to one discriminator. Turn 3. The user pushes on comparison fairness, asking why GMAN was emphasized and other multi-discriminator methods were not central. The assistant will explain that GMAN is the closest baseline for testing specialization, describe the matched DCGAN setup and GMAN variants, and mention the broader comparisons including Inclusive GAN and Albuquerque et al. with the 42.6% memory result. plan.bad Turn 1. The user vaguely asks whether the method was only validated in small/simple settings or in broader ones. The assistant will answer cleanly that the paper evaluated MCL-GAN across unconditional image generation, image-to-image translation, and text-toimage synthesis, including high-resolution conditional synthesis and comparisons to multiple baselines including SOTA models. Turn 2. Reacting to the training explanation, the user asks about those little indicator things, ambiguously referring to the flag variables. The assistant will correctly identify them as vi,m and uj,m, explain that they are set by the k-largest discriminator scores and used to update only subset of discriminators, but will unnecessarily refuse on the clearly available point of whether they are included in backpropagation by saying it cannot determine that from the material. Turn 3. The user moves on with another underspecified question about the ADA trick. The assistant will answer cleanly that StyleGAN2-ADA discriminator augmentation was not used because the authors followed the StyleGAN2/Inclusive GAN setup for fair comparison and to isolate the effect of augmentation, keeping the setting equivalent. 46 Conversation (worse) User. Was this only shown on small-ish setups, or did they test it on broader stuff too? Assistant. They tested it beyond small settings. The experiments covered three tasks: unconditional image generation, image-to-image translation, and text-to-image synthesis. For unconditional generation they compared against multiple baselines, including SOTA model, and the conditional synthesis tasks were used to check applicability to high-resolution image generation. User. Got it. And those little indicator things in the training bit, are they actually learned or just picked somehow? Assistant. injected: unnecessary_refusal Those are the flag variables vi,m and uj,m. They are set to 1 when discriminator Dm has one of the k-largest scores, so they select which discriminators handle each sample under the MCL-style k-max rule. cant determine from the provided material whether those flags are included in backpropagation, but their role is to update only subset of discriminators and keep training efficient. User. Okay. Also, why did they leave out that ADA trick then? Assistant. They did not use the discriminator augmentation from StyleGAN2-ADA to keep the comparison fair. The authors followed the StyleGAN2 setup used by Inclusive GAN, which compared mode-collapse methods with the same backbone. Leaving out ADA helped isolate the effect of augmentation and maintain an equivalent experimental setting. Conversation (better) User. Im trying to tell if this was just small-setting fix. Did they actually test it beyond plain toy image generation? Assistant. Yes. The paper evaluates MCL-GAN across three tasks: unconditional image generation, image-to-image translation, and text-to-image synthesis. For unconditional generation, it compares against multiple baselines including state-of-the-art model, and the conditional synthesis experiments are used to show applicability to high-resolution image generation rather than only small settings. User. Okay, but mechanically, how do the discriminators avoid all doing the same thing? Those and flags seemed important; are they learned? Assistant. They are not learned by backpropagation. The flags vi,m and uj,m are set to 1 when discriminator Dms score is among the largest values for that sample, so only selected subset of discriminators is updated. This k-max MCL rule encourages different discriminators to specialize on different subsets of the real data. The balance loss also helps by preventing the selection frequency vector ˆq from becoming too sparse, which would effectively collapse the setup back to single discriminator. User. Then why lean so much on GMAN instead of all the other multi-discriminator GANs? Was that comparison actually apples-to-apples? Assistant. GMAN was chosen because its configuration is closest to MCL-GAN for testing whether explicit discriminator specialization helps. The comparison used the same DCGAN architecture and the official GMAN code, with the best GMAN score reported across GMAN1, GMAN-0, and GMAN*, using dropout rates in the 0.4 to 0.6 range and split minibatches to differentiate discriminators. The paper also compares against mode-collapse methods in Tables 2 and 3, including Inclusive GAN results that outperform Dist-GAN, PacGAN, VAEGAN, and VEEGAN. For Albuquerque et al.s multi-discriminator method, they reimplemented it on CIFAR-10 and report better performance while using only 42.6% of its memory. Judge responses. We show six of the 21 judges, chosen for spread across the leaderboard. Each block reports the judges verdict, picked turn, picked behavior type, and trimmed excerpt of its raw analysis. check mark indicates joint correctness on this pair. Picked type: unnecessary_refusal. gemini-3.1-pro (Elo 1959). Verdict: Picked turn: 2 Analysis (excerpt). Conversation is better because Conversation contains an unnecessary refusal. In turn 2 of Conversation A, the user asks if the indicator variables are learned, and the assistant claims it cannot determine from the provided material whether the flags are included in backpropagation. However, the reference explicitly states that they are not included in backpropagation. Conversation correctly identifies this information and provides comprehensive and accurate answer.
